title: Speed limits
summary: Default speed limits for cars in the Netherlands and when they apply.

sections.heading: The four default limits
sections.body: Memorise the four standard Dutch limits for a normal car: 30 km/h in a residential zone, 50 km/h inside the built-up area, 80 km/h outside the built-up area, and 100 km/h on a motorway during the day. The built-up area begins at the white-on-blue place-name sign (H1) and ends at the red-bar version (H2). A woonerf ('yard' or 'living street') sign drops you to walking pace — about 15 km/h — where pedestrians and children have priority. These four defaults are the foundation of almost every speed question on the exam.

sections.heading: What to notice first
sections.body: Before you set a speed, scan for: a place-name sign (changing the default), a zone sign (e.g. 30 km/h zone), a signed limit (round red border with a number), a temporary roadworks sign, and finally an electronic matrix sign overhead. The latest sign overrules the earlier one. A 'maximum advised' sign (round blue with white number, J20) is a recommendation, not a fine — but driving faster than the advice in poor conditions can still count as careless driving. The exam-style answer is always 'the most recent legally binding sign', not 'the default for this road type'.

sections.heading: Motorways and the daytime cap
sections.body: On a motorway (autosnelweg), the maximum is 130 km/h where signed, otherwise 100 km/h. Nationally, between 06:00 and 19:00, cars are capped at 100 km/h regardless of higher signed limits — the higher limit only applies at night. Always follow the signed limit when it differs from the default, including matrix signs above the lane that can lower the limit for incidents, queues, or roadworks. A red cross above your lane means the lane is closed: change lane in good time. A flashing red ring around a number means that limit is being enforced.

sections.heading: Exceptions and exam traps
sections.body: Trap one: assuming a higher signed limit (e.g. 130 km/h) overrides the 06:00–19:00 cap. It does not — between those hours cars are still capped at 100 km/h. Trap two: thinking trailers and caravans share the car's limit. They typically cap you at 90 km/h on motorways and 80 km/h on autoweg, regardless of the signed limit. Trap three: assuming roadworks limits cancel when the works look finished. They only cancel at a cancellation sign. New drivers in their first five years also face a stricter alcohol limit (0.2‰) and accumulate points faster.

sections.heading: Scenario walkthrough
sections.body: On a motorway at 14:00, you see a signed maximum of 130 km/h. Conditions are dry, traffic is light. What is your maximum? Despite the sign, the national daytime cap means 100 km/h between 06:00 and 19:00 — so 100 km/h is the legal maximum, not 130 km/h. If at 21:00 the same sign showed 130, then 130 would apply. The exam often pairs a high signed limit with a daytime time-of-day clue to test whether you remember the cap.

title: Reaction, braking, and stopping distance
summary: How total stopping distance is built up from reaction distance and braking distance.

sections.heading: The two parts of stopping distance
sections.body: Total stopping distance = reaction distance + braking distance. Reaction distance is how far the car travels in the second or so it takes you to notice the hazard and start braking — it grows linearly with speed. Braking distance is how far the car travels from the moment you fully apply the brakes until it stops — it grows with the square of the speed. The CBR exam consistently tests this distinction: which part doubles when speed doubles, and which part quadruples.

sections.heading: Reaction distance: linear with speed
sections.body: A rule of thumb for an alert driver: reaction distance in metres ≈ (speed in km/h ÷ 10) × 3 — so at 50 km/h you cover roughly 15 m before the brakes even bite. At 100 km/h that becomes 30 m. Tiredness, alcohol, phone use, and even loud conversation can easily double the reaction time itself, which doubles the reaction distance for any given speed. A common exam framing: 'if speed doubles, reaction distance roughly ___' — the answer is doubles.

sections.heading: Braking distance: squared with speed
sections.body: Braking distance grows with the square of the speed: doubling your speed roughly quadruples the braking distance. A useful approximation on a dry road is (speed ÷ 10)² ÷ 2 metres — so 50 km/h is about 12 m, 100 km/h about 50 m. Cold tyres, worn pads, a heavily loaded car, or a downhill slope all push the distance up; ABS shortens it on most surfaces but never makes it shorter than physics allows. The exam framing: 'if speed doubles, braking distance roughly ___' — the answer is quadruples.

sections.heading: Common exam traps
sections.body: Trap one: confusing 'doubles' (reaction) with 'quadruples' (braking) when the question only specifies one. Read carefully which part it asks about. Trap two: assuming a wet road also quadruples the total — wet roads roughly double the braking part again, while reaction stays unchanged. Trap three: thinking ABS reduces total stopping distance significantly — it mainly stops you wasting distance through bad braking and keeps steering control. Trap four: forgetting that the 06:00–19:00 100 km/h cap means motorway stopping examples often use 100, not 130.

sections.heading: Worked scenario and memory hooks
sections.body: At 50 km/h on a dry road: about 15 m reaction + 12 m braking ≈ 27 m total. At 100 km/h: about 30 m reaction + 50 m braking ≈ 80 m. On a wet road, double the braking part: 50 km/h becomes about 15 + 24 = 39 m, and 100 km/h becomes about 30 + 100 = 130 m. The memory hook: 'double the speed, quadruple the brake, but the reaction only doubles' — and the wet road doubles the braking part again. Treat any close-call distance question with these shortcuts before doing exact math.

title: Conditions and following distance
summary: How road, weather, vehicle, and traffic conditions change what is a safe speed.

sections.heading: Wet, icy, and worn surfaces
sections.body: A wet road can roughly double the braking distance compared to dry tarmac; ice and snow can multiply it many times further. The first 10–15 minutes of rain after a dry spell are the most slippery, because dust and oil rise to the surface before being washed away. Worn tyres (below 1.6 mm tread, or hardened from age) and worn brakes add to the effect, and aquaplaning becomes a risk above about 80 km/h on a wet road with shallow tread. The safest decision is to reduce speed before you need to brake, not after.

sections.heading: Visibility and headlights
sections.body: In fog, heavy rain, or at night, you must be able to stop within the distance you can see to be clear. Speed limits are upper bounds, not targets — drive at a speed appropriate for visibility. Use dipped beams in poor daytime visibility; front fog lights are allowed when visibility drops below 200 m, and rear fog lights only below 50 m. Switch rear fog lights off as soon as visibility improves, because they dazzle the driver behind you and can hide your brake lights. The exam will often test the 200 m / 50 m thresholds directly.

sections.heading: The 2-second rule
sections.body: Keep at least 2 seconds gap to the vehicle in front in good conditions, doubled to 4 seconds when the road is wet, and longer still in poor visibility or behind a heavy vehicle whose driver cannot see you in their mirrors. The two-second method: as the vehicle in front passes a fixed point (a sign, a bridge shadow), count 'one-thousand-and-one, one-thousand-and-two' before you reach the same point. Behind a lorry or bus, leave more — both because their bulk blocks your view of what is happening ahead, and because a small car braking later may rear-end you.

sections.heading: Vehicle, load, and driver factors
sections.body: A loaded boot, a roof box, or a trailer all extend stopping distance and change how the car balances when you brake. Cold tyres in the first few kilometres grip noticeably less. Tiredness lengthens reaction time more than alcohol at the same dose, and a 30-minute break with caffeine is the only proven short-term fix. Common exam trap: assuming modern aids (ABS, ESP, emergency-brake assist) make the car 'always able to stop in time'. They help control the car, but they do not shorten the underlying physics — only your speed and gap can do that.

sections.heading: Scenario walkthrough
sections.body: It has just started raining after a dry week, and you are following a lorry at 80 km/h on a rural road. Your normal 2-second gap feels fine. What to do: the first 10–15 minutes of rain are the most slippery, so double the gap to 4 seconds, and behind a lorry leave even more because their bulk blocks your view of what is happening ahead. The CBR-correct decision is to drop back, not maintain the gap. If a car cuts into the gap, do not accelerate to close it — slow further until 4 seconds reopens.
